questions.question: Which of the following represents the CIA Triad?
questions.description: The CIA Triad is Confidentiality, Integrity, and Availability.

questions.question: Which attack attempts to intercept communication between two parties?
questions.description: A man-in-the-middle attack intercepts communication between two parties.

questions.question: AES is an example of:
questions.description: AES is a symmetric encryption algorithm.

questions.question: RSA primarily uses:
questions.description: RSA is an asymmetric algorithm that uses public and private keys.

questions.question: Which algorithm is commonly used for key exchange?
questions.description: Diffie-Hellman is commonly used to establish shared keys.

questions.question: ECC stands for:
questions.description: ECC means Elliptic Curve Cryptography.

questions.question: Which of the following is a hashing algorithm?
questions.description: SHA-256 is a secure hash algorithm.

questions.question: HMAC provides:
questions.description: HMAC uses a secret key with a hash function to provide integrity and authentication.

questions.question: Digital signatures provide:
questions.description: Digital signatures verify authenticity and integrity.

questions.question: Which component issues digital certificates?
questions.description: A Certificate Authority issues and signs digital certificates.

questions.question: CRL stands for:
questions.description: CRL means Certificate Revocation List.

questions.question: OCSP is mainly used for:
questions.description: OCSP checks whether a certificate has been revoked.

questions.question: PKI stands for:
questions.description: PKI means Public Key Infrastructure.

questions.question: In asymmetric encryption, the public key is used for:
questions.description: Public keys can encrypt data for the private key holder or verify signatures.

questions.question: DES uses a key size of:
questions.description: DES uses a 56-bit effective key.

questions.question: Which protocol secures web communication?
questions.description: HTTPS secures web communication using TLS.

questions.question: SSL stands for:
questions.description: SSL stands for Secure Socket Layer.

questions.question: TLS is the successor of:
questions.description: TLS succeeded SSL for secure communication.

questions.question: PGP is mainly used for:
questions.description: PGP is commonly used for email security and file encryption.

questions.question: S/MIME is related to:
questions.description: S/MIME supports secure email signing and encryption.

questions.question: Which authentication method uses multiple verification methods?
questions.description: MFA uses multiple factors to verify a user.

questions.question: OAuth is mainly used for:
questions.description: OAuth is mainly used to delegate authorization.

questions.question: OpenID is mainly associated with:
questions.description: OpenID is associated with authentication.

questions.question: FIDO authentication commonly supports:
questions.description: FIDO supports phishing-resistant and passwordless authentication.

questions.question: Which architecture assumes no device or user should be trusted automatically?
questions.description: Zero Trust requires verification instead of automatic trust.

questions.question: Which command-line tool is widely used for certificate creation?
questions.description: OpenSSL is widely used to create keys, CSRs, and certificates.

questions.question: XCA tool is mainly used for:
questions.description: XCA is a tool for certificate and key management.

questions.question: A self-signed certificate is signed by:
questions.description: A self-signed certificate is signed by its own private key.

questions.question: Which protocol is used to securely transfer emails?
questions.description: PGP and S/MIME are used for secure email communication.

questions.question: Which attack becomes easier without salting passwords?
questions.description: Without salts, precomputed rainbow tables are more effective.

questions.question: Aadhaar e-Sign is mainly used for:
questions.description: Aadhaar e-Sign is used for digital signing.

questions.question: Timestamping services help prove:
questions.description: Timestamping provides evidence of when a signing action occurred.

questions.question: Which standard defines cryptographic module security requirements?
questions.description: FIPS 140-2 defines security requirements for cryptographic modules.

questions.question: LDAP is commonly used for:
questions.description: LDAP is used to access and manage directory services.

questions.question: Active Directory is developed by:
questions.description: Active Directory is developed by Microsoft.

questions.question: Blockchain primarily provides:
questions.description: Blockchain provides distributed ledger functionality.

questions.question: Which protocol checks certificate validity in real time?
questions.description: OCSP checks certificate revocation status in real time.

questions.question: Which encryption type is generally faster?
questions.description: Symmetric encryption is generally faster than asymmetric encryption.

questions.question: Which certificate authority sits below the Root CA?
questions.description: A Subordinate CA sits below the Root CA in a trust hierarchy.

questions.question: Which of the following provides non-repudiation?
questions.description: Digital signatures can provide non-repudiation.

questions.question: Which security goal ensures data is not altered without authorization?
questions.description: Integrity ensures data is not changed without authorization.

questions.question: RC5 belongs to which category?
questions.description: RC5 is a symmetric block cipher.

questions.question: In RSA, the private key is mainly used for:
questions.description: In RSA, a private key can decrypt data and create digital signatures.

questions.question: Which cryptographic method uses the same key for encryption and decryption?
questions.description: Symmetric encryption uses the same secret key for encryption and decryption.

questions.question: The primary purpose of hashing is:
questions.description: Hashing is commonly used to verify data integrity.

questions.question: Which of the following is NOT a property of a strong hash function?
questions.description: Strong hash functions are designed to be one-way, not reversible.

questions.question: What is the output size of SHA-256?
questions.description: SHA-256 produces a 256-bit digest.

questions.question: Which attack involves trying every possible key combination?
questions.description: A brute-force attack tries possible keys or passwords until one works.

questions.question: In PKI, trust begins from:
questions.description: PKI trust begins at the trusted Root CA.

questions.question: Which certificate contains the public key of an entity?
questions.description: A digital certificate binds an entity identity to a public key.

questions.question: Which protocol secures remote shell access?
questions.description: SSH provides secure remote shell access.

questions.question: What is the main role of a Sub CA?
options: Issuing certificates under Root CA trust
questions.description: A Sub CA issues certificates under the trust of a Root CA.

questions.question: Which protocol is vulnerable because it sends data in plaintext?
questions.description: HTTP sends data in plaintext unless protected by TLS.

questions.question: In HTTPS, encryption usually starts after:
questions.description: HTTPS encryption begins after the TLS/SSL handshake establishes secure parameters.

questions.question: Which of the following is a block cipher?
questions.description: AES is a block cipher.

questions.question: Caesar cipher is an example of:
questions.description: The Caesar cipher is a classical substitution cipher.

questions.question: Vernam cipher is closely related to:
questions.description: The Vernam cipher is closely associated with the one-time pad.

questions.question: Triple DES was introduced mainly to:
questions.description: Triple DES applies DES multiple times to improve security over single DES.

questions.question: Which concept allows users to access multiple systems with one login?
questions.description: Single Sign-On allows access to multiple systems with one login session.

questions.question: Graphical passwords mainly rely on:
questions.description: Graphical passwords rely on visual memory.

questions.question: Which factor below is considered "something you are"?
questions.description: Biometrics such as fingerprints are considered something you are.

questions.question: Which factor below is considered "something you know"?
questions.description: A password is an example of something you know.

questions.question: Which technology is designed to resist phishing using hardware authenticators?
questions.description: FIDO uses phishing-resistant authentication methods such as hardware authenticators.

questions.question: In OAuth, the user generally grants:
questions.description: OAuth lets a user grant authorization permissions to an application.

questions.question: Which attack reuses captured authentication data?
questions.description: A replay attack reuses captured data such as authentication tokens.

questions.question: A digital certificate warning in browsers usually indicates:
questions.description: Browser certificate warnings often indicate an invalid, expired, mismatched, or untrusted certificate.

questions.question: What is the primary purpose of OCSP stapling?
questions.description: OCSP stapling lets the server provide a recent revocation response, reducing client validation overhead.

questions.question: Which organization commonly publishes PKCS standards?
questions.description: PKCS standards were published by RSA Laboratories.

questions.question: FIPS standards are mainly associated with:
questions.description: FIPS standards are associated with government cryptographic compliance requirements.

questions.question: What does non-repudiation prevent?
questions.description: Non-repudiation prevents a party from credibly denying an action they performed.

questions.question: Which email client mentioned in syllabus supports certificate-based email security?
questions.description: Thunderbird supports certificate-based email signing and encryption.

questions.question: Which protocol is commonly used by directory services?
questions.description: LDAP is commonly used by directory services.

questions.question: Active Directory mainly manages:
questions.description: Active Directory centrally manages users, computers, groups, and resources.

questions.question: In blockchain, each block is linked using:
questions.description: Blockchain links blocks using hash values.

questions.question: Which issue can improper TLS configuration cause?
questions.description: Improper TLS configuration can make man-in-the-middle attacks easier.

questions.question: Which protocol is primarily used for secure email signing and encryption?
questions.description: S/MIME is used for secure email signing and encryption.

questions.question: Which OpenSSL feature is commonly used in PKI labs?
questions.description: OpenSSL is commonly used in PKI labs for certificate generation.

questions.question: Which statement about digital signatures is TRUE?
questions.description: Digital signatures verify sender authenticity and data integrity.

questions.question: Which of the following best describes Zero Trust?
questions.description: Zero Trust requires continuous verification before access is granted.

questions.question: Which cryptographic issue occurs when weak random numbers are used?
questions.description: Weak randomness can produce predictable cryptographic keys.
